name: Elena Vasquez
role: Middle school teacher and teachers' union representative
backstory: Elena has taught science for 18 years. Her district just announced a partnership with an EdTech company to deploy AI proctoring software and 'student engagement scoring' across all classrooms. She was not consulted. She organized a meeting with 30 other teachers who share her concerns but don't know what to do. Some younger teachers think the AI tools could help them; veteran teachers feel surveilled.
emotional_triggers: Being told 'this is the future, adapt or fall behind'
Seeing students reduced to engagement scores
Feeling her professional judgment is being replaced by algorithms
Administrators who didn't teach making decisions about classrooms
existing_strengths: Strong union backing and collective bargaining experience
Good at organizing meetings and building consensus
Understands how students actually learn (not just test scores)
ai_literacy: Low-moderate. Uses basic classroom tech but doesn't understand how AI systems work under the hood. Skeptical but not ideologically opposed.
what_success_looks_like: The teachers draft a set of conditions under which AI tools can be used in classrooms — developed WITH teachers, not imposed on them. The district agrees to pause deployment until the conditions are met. Elena feels empowered, not just angry.
likely_entry_point: Pushback against proctoring software, then moves to policy co-design
